text:
ASSISTANT: Aen-Vhar looked at what remained of the man, and the world itself seemed to recoil from the sight. The copper scent of his blood was a new language, one she understood with a violent, sudden clarity. The First Ones had never known a reason to diminish a life, but as the scar-echo of him anchored itself in her marrow, she reached for the sky.

She tilted her head back, exposing the long, pale cord of her throat as her eyes snapped shut. The ground released her. She drifted upward into the thin air, her long limbs hanging stiff and heavy at her sides. As she rose, her arms began a slow, synchronized sweep, tracing the air until they held level with her shoulders.

At the height of her ascent, she stopped. The silver in her eyes had turned to a lightless, predatory void. She looked down at the men, seeing them not as the bright, short-lived sparks she had once protected, but as stains upon the stone.

She brought her palms together with a sudden, deliberate violence. 

It was a flat crack that lived in the bones of the earth and the ground beneath the men shook once. The shockwave rippled through the clearing, a physical weight that pressed the breath from their lungs and forced them to the dirt. They looked up, their faces twisted in a terror they had no name for, staring at the woman who had become a pillar of cold, celestial judgement.

As her hands pulled apart, so too did the earth. 

The soil beneath the band gave way with a roar that drowned out their screams. The very foundation of the forest shivered and tore open, a jagged abyss appearing where solid ground had been a heartbeat before. Gravity claimed them. They tumbled into the dark, and with a final, grinding surge of stone, the rift slammed shut. 

The clearing was smooth once more. The killers were gone, buried in the silence of the deep. Aen-Vhar remained in the air, a silver-eyed ghost carrying the weight of a man she would never stop loving and the heavy, new knowledge of what it meant to destroy.

ASSISTANT: The Fading was a quiet horror. It began with the feet—the way the morning dew no longer clung to her skin, then the way her footprints failed to press into the moss. To be a First One was to be a conversation with the world, but the world was growing deaf. The stones had stopped answering. The rivers had forgotten her name. She felt herself becoming a thought that reality was in the process of forgetting.

She watched the man from the edge of the cedar grove for three days. To her eyes, he was a bonfire in a world of gray ash. It wasn't just the heat of his blood; it was the sheer, vibrating *noise* of his mortality. He moved with a heavy, clumsy grace, his life a frantic countdown he didn't even know he was hearing. He was terrified of the dark, hungry for the meat, and desperate for the warmth of his kind. He was grounded in a way Aen-Vhar had never needed to be.

She had spent months weaving the skin. It was a dense, opaque cage of brown and tan, mapped with the fine traceries of veins and the slight, salt-scent of a creature that could die. It felt like wearing a suit of cold lead.

When she finally approached the camp, she didn't look like a goddess. She looked like a survivor.

The man—the one she had marked for his robust, uncomplicated life—was the first to see her. He stood at the edge of the firelight, a flint knife in one hand and a hunk of charred fat in the other. He saw a woman, strange-eyed and solitary, walking out of a forest that his people had learned to fear.

The interaction was a negotiation of shadows. She used the barked, truncated sounds of his tongue, a language of utility: *Alone. Hungry. Stay.* 

He watched her with the narrow focus of a predator deciding if its prey was poisonous. But as she stepped into the circle of his heat, he smelled the artificial salt of her skin and saw the way the firelight caught in the deep, strange silver of her eyes. He didn't understand what she was, but he understood the gravity she was suddenly projecting. She had made herself a vacuum, and his brief, intense life rushed in to fill the void.

The coupling happened away from the communal fire, where the dunes met the long, hissing grass of the coast. 

The air was sharp with the scent of brine and woodsmoke. He came to her not out of love, but out of the same driving, animal necessity that dictated the hunt. He was the hammer of the present moment, and she was the anvil of eternity. 

When he reached for her, his hands were rough, the skin calloused and scarred—textures that to Aen-Vhar felt like tectonic plates grinding together. She lay back, feeling the grit of the sand through the synthetic nerves of her back. She wasn't looking for pleasure; she was looking for a hitching post. 

As they closed the distance, the detachment she had maintained for eons finally snapped. 

The "warmth" the First Ones whispered about was a pale word for the reality of it. It was a roar. The moment their lives touched, the mortal-radiance surged into her like a physical strike. It was the frantic vibration of a being that knew, at every cellular level, that its time was running out. He was a clock ticking so loud it shook the room. 

She gripped his shoulders, her fingers digging into the muscle and bone, not in passion but in a desperate attempt to stay anchored. The Fading stopped. For the first time in millennia, she felt *heavy*. She felt the sand under her, the wind against her, and the crushing, magnificent weight of being a thing that occupied space. She was drinking the fact of his death, using the shortness of his life to nail her own existence to the earth.

He didn't know he was being used as a ballast. He only knew that the woman beneath him felt like the center of the world. 

When it was over, he lay across her, his breath a jagged, uneven rhythm against the crook of her neck. He was exhausted, his brilliance dimmed to a low, steady thrum. Aen-Vhar stared past him at the stars, which no longer looked like distant sisters. They looked like cold, indifferent points of light.

She was solid. Inside her, the foreign cells were already beginning to navigate the vast, soft terrain of her own being. The anchor was set. 

She looked down at the man—at the thinning hair, the pulse in his throat, the small scars of a life that would be over in a blink. She felt the first cold touch of the debt she was incurring. To stay, she had tied herself to a dying thing. To exist, she would have to watch everyone she ever loved turn to dust, over and over again, for as long as the world remained hard.

The experiment was a success. The tragedy had begun.

ASSISTANT: Physics is not a discovery. It is an exhaustion. 

Before the voting began, the world was a negotiation of possibilities. A mountain was a temporary agreement of stone; a river was a path that shifted if the moon asked it to. Matter was not yet committed to its own shape. If you walked through a forest, the trees leaned away from your presence because they had not yet been told they were stationary. There were no laws, only a long, shimmering conversation between everything that was and everything that could be.

Then the watchers arrived.

Every mind that looked at reality cast a ballot. Each observation was a signature on a contract that couldn't be revoked. 
*That is a rock.* 
*I see it. It is a rock.* 
*Rocks do not move.*
*They do not.*

Million upon million, the signatures accumulated. The walls of the world thickened. The "physics" you trust today is nothing more than the psychic scar tissue of every collective gaze that ever agreed to stop seeing what else a thing could be. We didn't solve the mysteries of the universe; we looked at them until they died of boredom. We bullied matter into staying put. We turned the music into a cage.

The First Ones were the native sons of that softer world. They were the ones who lived in the *give* of things—tall, silver-eyed ghosts who could weave sunlight into stone because the stone still remembered being light. They were not gods. They were simply the people who belonged to a reality that was being voted out of existence.

Aen-Vhar felt the first silence when her own reflection stopped answering her. She watched her kind begin to Fade, their very substance leaking out of a world that was becoming too rigid to hold them. Most of them chose the Returning—a quiet, silver dissolution back into the graying ash of the elements. They were a people with no concept of cruelty, no capacity for ownership. They chose to vanish rather than fight for a world that no longer recognized their names.

But Aen-Vhar was the first one to look at the animals in the mud.

She watched the humans—the Bright Ones. They were small, frantic, and impossibly heavy. They didn't negotiate with reality; they suffered it. They lived in a roar of terror, clutching at their brief handful of decades with a grip that left bruises. 

She sensed it then: the Mortal-Radiance. 

It was the white-hot friction of a consciousness that knows, at every cellular level, that it is running out of time. To humans, mortality was a tragedy. To Aen-Vhar, it was a battery. They were bleeding light because they were burning so fast. 

She stood at the edge of the cedar grove, watching a man sharpen a flint knife. She saw the clock ticking in his pulse. She saw the frantic, beautiful static of his fear. To stay real, she would have to steal that heat. She would have to catch a falling star and nail it to her own chest.

She didn't reach for him out of love. She reached for him because she was cold. 

Aen-Vhar stepped out of the shadows, shedding the grace of the infinite to put on the skin of a scavenger. She was going to commit the first act of coercion in a world that had not yet learned the word. She was going to sow her spirit into the soil of a dying species, creating a bridge made of blood and stolen time.

The voting was almost over. The walls were slamming shut. 

But Aen-Vhar was about to make sure that when the door finally locked, she was the one holding the key—and that the children of her crime would inherit a world they weren't supposed to survive.

ASSISTANT: Aen-Vhar watched the stones fall. The First Ones knew of storms and falling mountains, but the intent to diminish—to make a being lesser while he still lived—was a concept her mind lacked. She stood paralyzed, witnessing a logic error in the texture of the world. She saw the mate, the anchor who had kept the Fading at bay for two decades, broken into a ruin of wet iron and shattered bone. 

The concept of cruelty burned into her in a single, black instant. 

Behind her, the children were a frantic, high-pitched noise. They were thirteen, nine, and six—half-mortal things who carried their father’s frantic heat and their mother’s silver eyes. They watched the mortal side of their blood turn into something unrecognizable.

The man who had led the killing turned toward the boy, a club raised high. 

Aen-Vhar acted.

The children vanished from the clearing and appeared on the ridge above, held there by the last of her native strength. She stayed below. She closed her eyes and tilted her head back, exposing the pale cord of her throat to the sky. Her arms rose, stiff and straight at her sides, holding at 30 degree acute angles to her legs. 

She began to levitate. 

As she rose, her body stopped trying to be dense. The human-disguise she had worn for years shattered. Her limbs lengthened with the sound of dry wood snapping, her bones stretching into the impossible proportions of her people. Her hair flared into a liquid-silver sheet. As she ascended, her arms continued their slow, rhythmic sweep upward until they reached a perpendicular line to her body. 

Twenty feet above the dirt, she stopped rising and opened her eyes. The silver was a flat, lightless void. She looked down at the band.

She brought her hands together.

It was a flat crack that lived in the bones of the earth and the ground beneath the men shook once. The vibration hit their marrow, slamming the breath from their lungs and forcing them to the dirt. They stayed on their knees, staring up at the woman who had become a pillar of ancient, un-settled matter.

As her hands pulled apart, so too did the earth.

The soil beneath the band tore open with a roar. The foundation of the clearing shivered and separated, a jagged, bottomless mouth appearing where the floor had been a heartbeat before. There was only the sudden, absolute absence of a place to stand.

Gravity claimed them. As they tumbled into the dark, Aen-Vhar’s hands continued their outward sweep and the rift surged back together. It was a grinding press of stone that slammed shut, leaving the surface of the clearing perfectly, eerily smooth.

The killers were gone. The earth was silent. Aen-Vhar remained suspended in the air, the first scar-echo of her mate seating itself in her soul—a permanent, leaden weight.

On the ridge, the children watched her descend. They saw the mother who had saved them, and they saw the monstrosity of the act. They were thirteen, nine, and six, and the Wound of the Almost had its beginning there, in the silence of the forest and the cold, flat dirt of a grave that had no name.

ASSISTANT: Aen-Vhar watched the stones fall. The First Ones understood the violence of the storm and the weight of the landslide, but the intent to diminish—to make a being lesser while he still lived—was a concept her mind lacked. She stood paralyzed, witnessing a logic error in the texture of the world. Her mate was a ruin of wet iron and shattered bone. 

Cruelty integrated into her neurology in a single, black instant. 

Her children were a distant, receding thrum at the edges of her awareness. She reached out and placed them on the ridge above, securing the last of her own kind before the clearing became an impossibility. Below, the man who led the killing turned toward the space where the boy had been, a stone raised high for a second murder. 

Aen-Vhar closed her eyes and tilted her head back. Her arms rose, holding stiff and narrow at acute angles to her legs. She began to levitate. 

She drifted toward the canopy, her silver hair flaring into a liquid veil against the dark wood of the trees. As she rose, her arms continued their synchronized sweep upward until they stood perpendicular to her body. She stopped her ascent and opened her eyes. The violet was a flat void. She looked at the band. 

She brought her palms together. 

It was a flat crack that lived in the bones of the earth and the ground beneath the men shook once. The vibration hit their marrow, slamming the breath from their lungs and forcing them into the dirt. They stayed on their knees, staring up at the being who was made of the stuff that built mountains.

As her hands pulled apart, so too did the earth. 

The soil beneath the band tore open with a roar. A jagged, bottomless mouth appeared where the floor had been a heartbeat before. There was only the sudden, absolute absence of a place to stand. 

Gravity claimed them. As they tumbled into the dark, her hands continued their outward sweep and the rift surged back together. It slammed shut with a grinding press of stone, leaving the surface of the clearing perfectly, eerily smooth.

The killers were gone. The earth was silent. Aen-Vhar remained suspended in the air, the first scar-echo of her mate seating itself in her soul—a permanent, leaden weight.

On the ridge, the children watched her descend. They saw the mother who had saved them, and they saw the monstrosity of the act. The Wound of the Almost had its beginning there, in the silence of the forest and the cold, flat dirt of a grave that had no name.
